heroHeadline: Sheinbaum at 572 Days: CIA-Chihuahua Sovereignty Crisis Escalates — State AG Resigns, Governor Refuses Senate; Mexico Reaches Record 60.2M Employed Workers
heroSubtitle: The CIA-Chihuahua sovereignty confrontation dominated April 27–29 as President Sheinbaum's administration pursued accountability for unauthorized U.S. intelligence operations on Mexican soil. At her April 27 mañanera, Sheinbaum presented federal evidence and directly blamed Chihuahua state officials — naming Governor Maru Campos and State AG César Jáuregui Moreno as the only officials who could have authorized the CIA joint operation that killed two agents and two Mexican investigators on April 20. The LA Times reported at least three additional CIA operations inside Chihuahua in 2026 without federal notification, with agents disguised in AEI uniforms. On April 28, Jáuregui Moreno resigned — Sheinbaum responded: 'The investigation does not stop with a resignation' — while Governor Campos refused a Senate summons, declaring: 'It is not mandatory; we answer to our people, not the Senate.' The U.S. diplomatically replied that it 'wants to respect Mexico's law and Constitution.' The federal probe is now examining whether state officials violated Article 29 of Mexico's National Security Law. Meanwhile, a positive milestone: Labor Secretary Bolaños announced Mexico reached 60.2 million employed workers — a record and the OECD's lowest unemployment rate — with 422,000 new jobs year-over-year confirmed at the April 28 mañanera. The USMCA first official negotiating round remains on track for the week of May 25.
footerNote: Data sourced from Diario Oficial, INEGI, Banco de México, SNSP, Secretaría de Economía, INAH, Reuters, AP, El Universal, Reforma, Animal Político, Mexico News Daily, Infobae, Milenio, La Jornada, Al Jazeera, Bloomberg, AtlasIntel, USTR, Chatham House, Mexico Business News, El Financiero, La Silla Rota, Proceso, CNN Español, Lopez Doriga, The Yucatan Times, Excelsior, La Razón de México, Quinto Poder, PBS NewsHour, NBC News, Time, NPR, CBC, Japan Kantei, Japan MOFA, SDP Noticias, Eje Central, TVAzteca, UnoTV, CBS News, El Mañana de Nuevo Laredo, Bloomberg Línea, Canadian PMO, UPI, MLB.com, El Imparcial, Expansión Política, Heraldo Binario, FONADIN, Ámbito, The Washington Post
